tip: Before you start, pick one main way you will measure success, like how well the new hire works out after 3 months, not just how fast you fill the role.
why_it_works: Choosing one clear goal up front keeps you and the hiring manager pulling in the same direction.
evidence: 89% of organisations rank quality of hire as critical, only 25% feel confident measuring it

tip: Have a quick kickoff chat with the manager before you write the ad, and write down the must-have skills, a simple list of what a great hire looks like, and the target start date.
why_it_works: A short planning chat at the start helps you fill the role faster and find someone who fits.
evidence: Misalignment forces 41% more mid-search requisition changes and adds 38% to time-to-fill

tip: Try not to run more than five to seven open roles at once, and only add another after you close or drop a stale one.
why_it_works: Juggling too many roles at once makes it harder to fill any of them well.
evidence: Drop from 7 to 5.8 reqs per recruiter lifted close rate from 42% to 79% in two quarters

tip: Choose four to six places to post the role rather than posting everywhere, so your effort and budget are not spread too thin.
why_it_works: A focused set of channels matched to the role gets better results than posting everywhere.
evidence: 4 to 6 channels is the sweet spot across sourcing studies

tip: Decide up front that you will reply to every applicant within 48 hours, and set that standard before you go live.
why_it_works: Most applicants wait days to hear back, so a quick reply makes you stand out.
evidence: 3 to 5 day average first contact lag drives ghosting

tip: Aim to run your Australian campaign in late January to March or mid-July to September, when more people are looking for jobs.
why_it_works: More people search for jobs at these times, so your ad gets seen by more candidates.
evidence: Seasonal SEEK job-ad and search patterns

tip: Before you post the role outside, check with your own team and ask if anyone could step up or move across into it.
why_it_works: People already in the company can start sooner and tend to stay longer.
evidence: Companies with strong internal mobility see 2x increase in retention

tip: Before you write any copy, pick one or two real reasons you are a good place to work, then use them across every channel.
why_it_works: A couple of clear, honest reasons to join keep people engaged and help them stay.
evidence: Deloitte EVP measurement data

tip: Write your rejection email and your offer email on day one, so you are not scrambling to word them under pressure later.
why_it_works: Having these ready means you can reply quickly and kindly when it counts.
evidence: 61% of job seekers have been ghosted after an interview

tip: Set an end date for the campaign, not just a start date, because roles that drag on past 60 days tend to lose the manager's attention.
why_it_works: A clear deadline keeps everyone focused and stops the role from going stale.
evidence: Stage-aging is a top dashboard signal

tip: Add one short employee video showing a real day in the role, filmed simply on a phone rather than a polished production.
why_it_works: Posts shared by real employees get far more attention than polished brand content.
evidence: 8x engagement uplift for employee posts

tip: Name the hiring manager in the ad and link to their LinkedIn, because ads with a real person attached do better than faceless ones.
why_it_works: Putting a real name and face to the role builds trust and gets more people to apply.
evidence: Gen Z candidates trust employee voice over brand voice

tip: Cut buzzwords like world-class, dynamic and rockstar from the ad, and use plain, concrete words instead.
why_it_works: Vague praise feels empty and makes the ad less believable.
evidence: 53% of hires report ads differed significantly from reality

tip: Pick two things that make you a good place to work, like flexibility and growth, and repeat them everywhere instead of listing ten.
why_it_works: Saying the same couple of things across every channel is what makes the message stick.
evidence: 75% of HR leaders find internal EVP comms challenging

tip: Ask three current staff for one sentence each on why they stay, and use those quotes in the ad and on the careers page.
why_it_works: Real quotes from staff feel genuine and land better than generic culture copy.
evidence: Employee voice drives trust in candidate decision making

tip: Use a real photo of the team the new person will work with, not a stock photo.
why_it_works: Real photos feel more honest and get more clicks than stock images.
evidence: Authentic visuals lift career-site conversion

tip: Add one short video on the careers page from someone already doing a similar job.
why_it_works: A short video can noticeably lift how many visitors go on to apply.
evidence: On-page video conversion boost

tip: Be honest about the workload in the ad. If the role is busy, say so and explain the support that comes with it.
why_it_works: Heavy workload is the top reason people leave jobs in Australia, so candidates look out for it.
evidence: 26% of AU employee departures cite excessive workload

tip: Spell out exactly what flexible means rather than just using the word, so say the hybrid days, the hours and how much freedom people have.
why_it_works: Most ads claim to be flexible, so giving the real detail is what sets yours apart.
evidence: Flexibility specificity drives candidate interest

tip: Run a simple, regular employee content series instead of one big one-off campaign video.
why_it_works: Regular small posts keep building interest, while a single video fades fast.
evidence: Short-form video outperforms longer formats for completion and shares

tip: Keep the job description between 200 and 400 words, as this is where the most people tend to apply.
why_it_works: Ads in this length get the most applications, while very long ones lose people.
evidence: Appcast analysis of 10.2 billion clicks

tip: Use a short, clear job title of one to four words that people would actually search for, and avoid clever ones like Coding Ninja.
why_it_works: Short, plain titles get more clicks and are easier for candidates to find.
evidence: 1-3 word titles average 3.41 clicks vs 2.75 for 13+ words

tip: Start the ad with what the person will actually own in their first few months, not with the company's history.
why_it_works: Leading with the real work gets more applications and shows you have a clear plan.
evidence: Candidates rate concrete role detail above company puffery

tip: List only four to six must-have requirements, and put everything else under a clearly labelled nice-to-have list.
why_it_works: A short must-have list encourages more people, especially those who self-doubt, to apply.
evidence: Clear must-have labelling shifts gender ratio of applicants

tip: Show the pay range in the ad, so the right people apply and trust you more.
why_it_works: Pay is one of the first things candidates look for, and showing it cuts wasted applications.
evidence: Almost one in four job seekers say pay is the most important ad detail

tip: Swap words that lean masculine, like competitive, dominate and aggressive, for plainer words that mean the same thing.
why_it_works: Removing words that skew male brings in more women without putting men off.
evidence: 29 to 40% application uplift across studies

tip: Drop the degree requirement unless the role legally needs one, and ask for the actual skills instead.
why_it_works: Focusing on skills rather than degrees opens the role up to far more good candidates.
evidence: Skills-based hiring expands pools 6x overall

tip: Include three benefits that genuinely matter for this role rather than listing every perk you offer.
why_it_works: A few relevant benefits get more people to apply than a long list of everything.
evidence: Targeted benefits framing lifts ad performance

tip: Add one screening question to the ad to filter out people who clearly are not a fit, without making it a chore to apply.
why_it_works: A single well-chosen question helps you find the right people without scaring others off.
evidence: Screener questions can lift hire likelihood by 50%

tip: Write the ad as if you are talking directly to the candidate using you, rather than describing the role from the outside.
why_it_works: Talking directly to the reader feels like a conversation and gets more people to apply.
evidence: Direct candidate-facing copy lifts apply rates

tip: Add one sentence about how someone can grow or learn in the role.
why_it_works: The chance to grow matters more to most candidates than perks like free coffee.
evidence: Gen Z prioritise purpose and growth over traditional perks

tip: Start the ad with a sentence that names the problem this role is there to solve.
why_it_works: Leading with the real problem grabs attention and gets more people to click apply.
evidence: Outcome-led copy lifts engagement

tip: Use the exact same job title in the ad, on your careers page and wherever else it is posted.
why_it_works: Matching titles help your ad show up higher in searches on sites like Indeed, SEEK and Google.
evidence: Consistent titles outrank creative aliases

tip: Write the ad in plain, simple English and keep sentences short.
why_it_works: Simple wording brings in more applicants and is easier to read on a phone.
evidence: Plain language consistently lifts conversion in copy testing

tip: Post two versions of the job title for the first 48 hours, then keep whichever one gets more applicants.
why_it_works: The job title is the one wording change that shifts apply rates the most, so testing it pays off.
evidence: Title length and wording shift click-to-apply rates substantially

tip: Name the actual location in the ad instead of vague words like nationwide or flexible.
why_it_works: People search hard by location, so being specific brings you the right candidates.
evidence: Location specificity is a top job board filter

tip: Add a line telling people to apply even if they do not tick every single box.
why_it_works: This simple invite brings in more applicants from a wider range of people, without lowering quality.
evidence: Inclusive framing widens applicant pool

tip: In Australia, post on SEEK first for reach, then add LinkedIn for specialist roles and Indeed as a backup.
why_it_works: SEEK is where most Australian job seekers look, while LinkedIn helps you find harder-to-fill specialists.
evidence: SEEK applications per ad at record highs in August 2025

tip: Ask each of your team members for two people they would recommend in the first week.
why_it_works: Candidates who come through your team are far more likely to be hired and to stay.
evidence: Referrals deliver higher conversion and retention

tip: Offer a single, fair cash reward to any staff member whose recommendation gets hired.
why_it_works: A clear, fair reward gets people taking part without skewing their judgement.
evidence: Typical referral bonuses range 1k to 5k per successful hire

tip: At the start of a search, reach back out to strong runners-up from past roles.
why_it_works: These people already know your business, so they are quick to reach and often keen to come back.
evidence: Silver-medalist re-engagement consistently exceeds 50% response

tip: When you message a candidate, mention a specific project or post of theirs instead of sending the same note to everyone.
why_it_works: A personal message gets far more replies than a copy-paste one.
evidence: Personalisation triples cold outreach response rates

tip: Keep your message to a candidate short, around two or three sentences.
why_it_works: Short, to-the-point messages get more replies than long pitches.
evidence: Short-form outreach data from LinkedIn benchmarks

tip: Send your messages to candidates Tuesday to Thursday, either early morning or late afternoon.
why_it_works: Mid-week, near the start or end of the workday, tends to get the best response.
evidence: Outreach timing benchmarks

tip: Reach out to a candidate three ways, by LinkedIn, email and phone, over about two weeks.
why_it_works: Trying more than one channel gets you a lot more replies than sticking to just one.
evidence: Multi-channel sequences achieve 15 to 25% reply vs 5 to 10% single-channel

tip: Once you can see which job boards bring in good candidates, shift more of your budget to those.
why_it_works: Spending more where it is already working gets you more of the right candidates for your money.
evidence: Programmatic shows 51% better targeting and 48% better effectiveness

tip: For any role you hire for more than once, keep a list of past candidates and check in with them each month.
why_it_works: Having a warm list ready means the next opening fills faster and costs less.
evidence: Monthly nurture cadence is the recommended baseline

tip: Group your list of candidates by skill, location and how interested they are before you email them.
why_it_works: Sending the right message to the right group gets more replies and feels less like spam.
evidence: Granular segmentation is the strongest predictor of nurture engagement

tip: For Australian trade roles, post in trade-specific places as well as SEEK, since general boards bring fewer responses.
why_it_works: Trade and care workers are in short supply in Australia, so you need to advertise where they actually look.
evidence: 139 AU occupations on shortage list since 2021, heavy in trades and care

tip: Post your ad using Indeed Apply if you can, so people can apply easily from their phone.
why_it_works: An easy mobile apply means far more people finish their application instead of dropping out.
evidence: Indeed Apply benchmark

tip: For roles aimed at younger candidates, post one short TikTok or Reels video showing a real day in the job.
why_it_works: Younger candidates watch a lot of short video and trust real staff more than a polished pitch.
evidence: Short-form video receives up to 35% higher engagement than static posts

tip: Make it easy for staff to share the role by writing the LinkedIn post for them to copy and paste.
why_it_works: Posts shared by your own people get noticed and trusted more than posts from the company page.
evidence: Employee-shared content gets 8x engagement

tip: Reply to every application within two days, even if it is just an automatic message saying you got it.
why_it_works: A quick reply keeps candidates feeling respected and stops them drifting to other jobs.
evidence: Only 19% of candidates hear back inside 24 hours today

tip: In your reply, tell candidates what happens next and roughly when they will hear back.
why_it_works: Knowing the timeline keeps people calm and less likely to take another offer while they wait.
evidence: 42% of candidates want stronger recruiter communication

tip: Use a text message for anything urgent, like confirming an interview time.
why_it_works: Texts get read almost straight away, so urgent updates actually land.
evidence: SMS open and reply rates dwarf email

tip: When people apply, ask how they prefer to be contacted, by text, email or phone.
why_it_works: Reaching people the way they like keeps them engaged and means fewer no-shows.
evidence: Candidates getting texts rate experience 50% higher

tip: Always let someone know they were not successful within five days of their interview.
why_it_works: Leaving people in silence leads to bad reviews online that can hurt your future ads.
evidence: 55% of job seekers avoid companies after reading negative reviews

tip: When you turn down someone you interviewed, add one specific, kind comment from the panel.
why_it_works: A personal no protects your reputation and keeps strong runners-up open to future roles.
evidence: Candidate resentment is at record levels

tip: After each search wraps up, send candidates a quick survey asking how the experience was.
why_it_works: A simple rating tells you whether your hiring process is leaving people with a good impression.
evidence: Standard CNPS benchmark interpretation

tip: In that survey, ask one open question: what would have made this better? Then use the answers next time.
why_it_works: People's own words point out easy fixes you might never spot on your own.
evidence: Only 41% of companies currently ask for feedback

tip: Keep your interview process to four steps or fewer for most roles, and say so in the job ad.
why_it_works: Fewer rounds means good people are less likely to drop out before you can hire them.
evidence: 31% of candidates wait 2-3 weeks for first interview, driving disengagement

tip: Tell candidates what the interview will cover before the day, so they can prepare.
why_it_works: Prepared candidates show you their real ability, not just how calm they are on the spot.
evidence: Structured interviews improve quality of hire

tip: Put the interviewer's name and job title in the calendar invite so candidates know who they will meet.
why_it_works: Knowing who they are meeting helps candidates prepare and show up ready.
evidence: Candidate communication consistently ranks as top improvement area

tip: Make the offer by phone within two days of the final interview.
why_it_works: Moving fast means you reach them before another company does.
evidence: Time-to-hire up 24% since 2021, driving acceptance decline

tip: On the offer call, ask whether their current employer is likely to offer them more money to stay.
why_it_works: Talking it through early means fewer last-minute surprises when they decide.
evidence: Counter-offers account for ~10% of declines

tip: Send the written offer the same day you make the verbal one.
why_it_works: A written offer in hand makes the candidate feel settled and less likely to walk away.
evidence: Offer process speed predicts acceptance

tip: Before you start interviewing, write a simple list of what a great hire looks like and score every candidate against it.
why_it_works: Scoring everyone the same way helps you pick the right person and avoid going on gut feel.
evidence: Structured interviews improve quality of hire and reduce bias

tip: Walk every hiring manager through your scoring list before they interview anyone.
why_it_works: Even a quick briefing helps everyone judge candidates the same fair way.
evidence: 38% of hiring managers receive formal interview training

tip: For roles with lots of applicants, give a short skills test before you read CVs.
why_it_works: What someone can actually do tells you more than what is written on their CV.
evidence: Skills-first hiring delivers 50% lower time-to-hire and 89% better retention

tip: Keep any take-home task to 20 minutes or less.
why_it_works: Short tasks mean busy, strong candidates will actually finish them instead of dropping out.
evidence: Candidate experience research links task length to drop-off

tip: Ask every candidate for the same role the same set of questions.
why_it_works: Same questions means you can compare answers fairly and pick the best person.
evidence: Structured interviews improve calibration and outcomes

tip: Keep interview panels to three people at most.
why_it_works: Smaller panels make decisions faster without missing anything important.
evidence: Stage and panel count drive decision lag

tip: Have each interviewer write down their own scores before everyone talks it over.
why_it_works: Scoring alone first stops one loud voice from swaying the whole group.
evidence: Independent scoring reduces anchoring bias

tip: For each must-have skill, ask the candidate to describe a time they actually used it.
why_it_works: What someone has done before is a good guide to what they will do for you.
evidence: Structured behavioural interviews are the highest-validity interview type

tip: Only reject a CV against your written list of requirements, never on a hunch.
why_it_works: Sticking to your list keeps your choices fair and avoids accidentally ruling people out unfairly on things like age, gender or background.
evidence: AI and human screening both reproduce historical bias without criteria discipline

tip: Let AI draft your job ad and screening questions, then read every line yourself before it goes live.
why_it_works: AI saves you time, but a quick human check makes sure the wording is right and on-brand.
evidence: 74% of TA pros say AI makes hiring more efficient

tip: Tell candidates when AI is used to screen them, and offer a way to opt out.
why_it_works: Being open about AI builds trust with the people applying to you.
evidence: Gartner recommends opt-out paths for AI screening

tip: Check your AI screening results every few months to make sure it is not unfairly filtering out older or female candidates, and step in if it is.
why_it_works: AI can quietly repeat old biases, so a regular check keeps your hiring fair and legal.
evidence: Stanford 2025 study shows AI screeners favour older male candidates

tip: Use a chatbot for common questions, scheduling and basic checks, but hand over to a real person the moment things get tricky.
why_it_works: A chatbot saves you time on the simple stuff while people still get a human when they need one.
evidence: 38% of recruitment processes now use chatbots

tip: If you use recorded video questions, keep it to three to five short ones, not ten.
why_it_works: A short set is far less of a chore, so more good candidates will complete it.
evidence: Async video pre-screens cut screening time by 80%

tip: Let candidates record their video answers more than once.
why_it_works: A second take means you see their real ability, not just first-time nerves.
evidence: Async video can feel impersonal and intimidating without affordances

tip: Use a scheduling tool to let candidates book their own interview slots.
why_it_works: It saves you hours of back-and-forth and gets interviews booked faster.
evidence: Async video and scheduling tools save 15+ hours per recruiter weekly

tip: Roll out one new AI tool at a time, not several at once.
why_it_works: Taking it one step at a time means your team actually sticks with the new tool.
evidence: Bersin TA Revolution recommends sequenced AI rollout

tip: Never let AI make the final hiring call. Use it to sort and rank, but you decide.
why_it_works: A person making the final call keeps the decision fair and catches mistakes the AI misses.
evidence: Best practice is AI-augmented, human-decided screening

tip: Aim to invite high-volume applicants to a first interview within five days of them applying.
why_it_works: Most people give up and look elsewhere if they wait two or three weeks to hear back.
evidence: 31% of candidates wait 2-3 weeks for first interview today

tip: Block out interview times in your calendar for the next two weeks before the ad goes live.
why_it_works: Having slots ready means no scrambling for times once applications start coming in.
evidence: Scheduling lag is a top driver of time-to-hire

tip: Where you can, combine the first interview and the work sample into one step.
why_it_works: Fewer steps means a faster process and more people who say yes to your offer.
evidence: Time-to-hire correlates with offer acceptance

tip: Keep an eye on how long candidates sit at each step. The slowest step is the one to fix first.
why_it_works: Spotting the slow step shows you exactly where to speed things up.
evidence: Stage-aging is the top dashboard signal across recruiting tools

tip: For most Australian roles, aim to fill the position in under 32 days.
why_it_works: 32 days is the typical time it takes in Australia, so it is a realistic target to aim for.
evidence: Median time-to-hire 32 days in AU

tip: Keep all messages with each candidate in one place, like a shared inbox or your hiring system.
why_it_works: One thread per candidate means nothing gets lost and people get quicker replies.
evidence: Candidate comms ranks as top experience improvement area

tip: Once the ad is live, book all your interviews in the same week instead of spreading them over a fortnight.
why_it_works: Keeping interviews close together helps you compare people fairly and stops good candidates drifting away.
evidence: Stage-aging predicts both acceptance and quality

tip: Before you post the ad, get the pay range signed off by whoever holds the budget.
why_it_works: Pay hold-ups are a top reason good people say no, so sorting it early protects the offer.
evidence: Compensation accounts for ~40% of offer declines

tip: Write your offer letter and your rejection email ahead of time so they are ready to send.
why_it_works: Having the words ready means you can respond in minutes on the day instead of losing hours.
evidence: Offer speed predicts acceptance

tip: Share the role on LinkedIn from the hiring manager's own profile, not just the company page.
why_it_works: Posts from a real person reach more people and feel more trustworthy than a company-page post.
evidence: Employee-shared content has 8x engagement

tip: Post one piece of social content each week while the role is open, rather than everything at launch.
why_it_works: A steady drip of posts keeps applications coming in over time, unlike a single launch-day burst.
evidence: Recurring content compounds across campaign window

tip: Film any video upright and keep it under 60 seconds so it fits Reels, Shorts and TikTok.
why_it_works: Short, upright clips get watched all the way through and shared more than long or sideways ones.
evidence: Under-60-second video wins on completion

tip: Add captions to every video you post.
why_it_works: Most people watch with the sound off, especially on their phone, so captions get your message across.
evidence: Mobile-default sound-off behaviour drives caption ROI

tip: Reuse each interview clip, panel shot and team photo across your different channels for a month.
why_it_works: Getting more mileage from each piece saves time and money and keeps your name in front of people.
evidence: Consistent multi-surface presence lifts employer brand recall

tip: Pin the role to the top of your company LinkedIn page until it closes.
why_it_works: A pinned post is the first thing every visitor sees, so it gets you extra reach for free.
evidence: LinkedIn page optimisation tactic

tip: Link every social post straight to the application form, not to a general careers page.
why_it_works: Every extra click loses people, so a direct link means more of them actually apply.
evidence: Mobile drop-off rises with every click

tip: When you find a strong runner-up, save their details with the role, their score and a date to get back in touch.
why_it_works: Going back to a strong runner-up is the quickest, cheapest way to fill your next role.
evidence: Silver-medalist re-engagement converts at 50%+

tip: Every few months, email your saved candidates one role, one story and one company update.
why_it_works: A light, regular hello keeps these people interested without flooding their inbox.
evidence: Monthly to quarterly cadence is the recommended baseline

tip: When someone is turned down or declines, ask if they would like you to keep their details for future roles.
why_it_works: Asking first gives you a keener list of candidates and keeps you on the right side of their privacy.
evidence: Pool consent improves engagement

tip: Before looking outside, list the people already at your company who could step up into the role.
why_it_works: Internal moves fill roles far faster than hiring externally and help good people stay.
evidence: Internal hires fill in 10 to 15 days vs 42 days external

tip: When you email saved candidates, include their name, the role they applied for and an update that fits them.
why_it_works: A message that feels personal is the single best way to get a reply.
evidence: Personalised nurture lifts open and reply rates

tip: Save candidates by the skills they have, not by their job title.
why_it_works: Skills stay useful longer and help you spot the same person for similar roles down the track.
evidence: Skills-based matching lifts pool reach by up to 6x

tip: Decide on five numbers you will watch each week before the campaign starts, and stop there.
why_it_works: A short list keeps you acting on what matters; too many numbers just become noise.
evidence: Three to five KPIs reviewed weekly is the recommended cadence

tip: Each week, check how long the role is taking to fill, how long it takes to hire, and how many applicants reach interview, offer and acceptance.
why_it_works: These few numbers cover the whole journey and show you where people are getting stuck.
evidence: Standard recruitment dashboard composition

tip: Take a closer look if filling the role is taking longer than about 42 days, or 32 days in Australia.
why_it_works: Comparing your timing to a typical figure tells you when something needs fixing.
evidence: SHRM 42 day average and SmartRecruiters AU 32 day median

tip: Keep a tally of what each hire costs you, all in.
why_it_works: Knowing the real cost helps you decide where to spend and what to tell the budget holder.
evidence: SHRM 2025 non-executive CPH $5,475

tip: At 90 days, ask the manager one question about how well the new hire is working out: would you hire them again.
why_it_works: One simple question you ask every time is more reliable than a complicated scoring system.
evidence: 66% of TA pros use manager ratings for QoH

tip: Pair the manager's 90-day rating with whether the person is still there at 12 months to see how well your hire worked out.
why_it_works: Looking at both an early and a later sign gives you a truer picture than either on its own.
evidence: Two-category quality-of-hire framework

tip: Ask applicants, in their own words, where they first heard about the role.
why_it_works: Their answer shows which channel really brought them in, which a tick-box list often misses.
evidence: Source-of-hire reflects application channel, not influence

tip: Compare what each channel costs you per good hire, not just per application.
why_it_works: Cheap applications can turn into expensive hires, so cost per good hire is the honest measure.
evidence: Quality-weighted attribution outperforms volume-weighted

tip: Aim for one in three to four people you interview getting an offer; if it drops below one in seven, take a look at your ad or interview.
why_it_works: This ratio quickly shows whether the problem is who you are attracting or how you interview.
evidence: Industry average 15 to 25%, top performers 28 to 38%

tip: Aim for most people to say yes to your offer; if fewer than 7 in 10 accept, check your speed and your pay.
why_it_works: How many people accept is the clearest sign of whether your offer stage is working.
evidence: Healthy 85 to 90%, poor under 70%

tip: After each campaign, ask candidates how they would rate the experience.
why_it_works: Their feedback shows you the rough spots your own team cannot see from the inside.
evidence: Standard NPS interpretation

tip: When the campaign wraps up, jot down one thing to keep doing and one thing to change next time.
why_it_works: Small lessons add up across campaigns, while a quick note now beats trying to remember later.
evidence: Cadence-based review is the recommended dashboard discipline

tip: Watch how many applicants make it to interview; if it is very few, your ad or channels may need work.
why_it_works: This early ratio points to ad or channel problems before they cost you the whole campaign.
evidence: 2% applicant-to-interview industry average

tip: Keep an eye on how many people apply in the first week as your early warning sign.
why_it_works: A slow first week rarely picks up, so spotting it early lets you fix the ad or channels straight away.
evidence: Week-one volume predicts campaign outcome

tip: Once the ad is live, text applicants to confirm their interview slot within 30 minutes of booking it.
why_it_works: Almost all texts get read within minutes, so a quick confirmation means fewer people forget to show up.
evidence: SMS 98% open rate, 3-minute read time

tip: Make a quick five-minute call before each interview to explain what to expect, so nobody walks in cold.
why_it_works: A little friendly contact beforehand puts people at ease and means more candidates go on to get an offer.
evidence: Recruiter touch predicts candidate engagement

tip: For your final candidates, tell them by phone that they missed out before you send the rejection email.
why_it_works: A personal call softens the blow, protects your reputation, and keeps strong runners-up warm for next time.
evidence: Candidate resentment is at record levels in 2024

tip: Within a day of someone accepting, send a written start date and a warm welcome note from their new manager.
why_it_works: A quick, friendly touch after acceptance makes people far less likely to back out before day one.
evidence: Offer-to-start gap is a known reneging window

tip: Keep your application form to under five minutes to complete, including uploading a resume.
why_it_works: Short forms get far more people to actually finish and apply than long ones.
evidence: Forms under 5 minutes convert at 12.5% vs 3.6% for forms over 15 minutes

tip: Cut your application form down to ten required questions or fewer.
why_it_works: Every extra question you ask for makes more people give up partway through, so fewer is better.
evidence: 4-to-3-field reduction lifts conversion 50%, 11-to-4 lifts 120%

tip: Before you go live, test the whole application yourself on a phone to make sure it works smoothly.
why_it_works: Most people apply from their phone, so a clunky mobile form quietly loses you good candidates.
evidence: Mobile applicants drop off 30-50% more on non-mobile-first apply flows

tip: Let people upload a resume file or pull their details from LinkedIn instead of retyping everything.
why_it_works: Typing on a phone is painful, so making it easy keeps more candidates from quitting the form.
evidence: Mobile applicants take 80% longer per field

tip: If your form has several steps, add a progress bar so people can see how close they are to finishing.
why_it_works: Knowing they are nearly done keeps more people going to the end instead of giving up.
evidence: 3 to 4 step forms with progress indicators outperform

tip: Never ask for the same detail twice; pull their name and email straight from the resume they uploaded.
why_it_works: Repeating questions annoys people and is a common reason they abandon the form halfway.
evidence: Form duplication kills completion

tip: Decide if you need lots of applicants or a few specialists, then pick channels to match: SEEK and Indeed for volume, LinkedIn and direct outreach for specialists.
why_it_works: Posting where the right people actually look gets you better candidates than spreading yourself thin.
evidence: LinkedIn 25% interview-to-offer for specialist; Indeed 76% hires for volume

tip: If you are hiring in AU healthcare, education, construction, engineering or science, plan for a longer search from the start.
why_it_works: These fields have ongoing staff shortages in Australia, so being patient up front saves stress later.
evidence: Most affected AU sectors per JSA 2025

tip: If local candidates are scarce, talk with your team early about whether you would consider hiring from overseas.
why_it_works: Most Australian employers already do this for hard-to-fill roles, so planning for it beats being caught short.
evidence: 60% of AU employers use overseas talent for shortages

tip: Before you start, set a target for how long it takes to fill the role: around 32 days is normal in Australia.
why_it_works: Having a realistic goal up front tells you whether your hiring is running ahead of or behind the pack.
evidence: AU benchmarks per SmartRecruiters 2025

tip: Back up every claim in your ad with proof: if you say flexible hours, show the roster; if you say career growth, show a real promotion path.
why_it_works: Concrete examples are far more convincing than vague promises, and candidates can tell the difference.
evidence: 53% of candidates report ads differing from reality

tip: Every few months, read your reviews on Glassdoor, SEEK and Google the way a candidate would before applying.
why_it_works: People check these before they apply, and bad reviews can quietly scare off good candidates.
evidence: 55% of job seekers avoid companies after reading negative reviews

tip: Reply calmly and specifically to any recent negative reviews about working for you.
why_it_works: A thoughtful reply shows you care; staying silent makes the complaint look true.
evidence: Review responsiveness drives perceived employer credibility

tip: Have a quick look on LinkedIn for good former staff who left on good terms and might come back.
why_it_works: People who already know the job settle in fast and need less training, so they are a quick, reliable win.
evidence: Passive sourcing studies favour warm networks

tip: For niche technical roles, find candidates in relevant communities and meet-ups before you turn to job boards.
why_it_works: People are far more likely to reply when you reach them somewhere they already trust.
evidence: Warm outreach reply rates 40-60%

tip: Note which channel each shortlisted person came from, not just where your final hire came from.
why_it_works: It shows you which job boards and channels reliably bring in strong candidates, so you spend where it pays off.
evidence: Multi-touchpoint attribution beats last-touch

tip: Ask an AI tool to draft three quick profiles of your ideal candidate before you start the campaign.
why_it_works: It is faster than starting from a blank page and helps your team agree on who you are really after.
evidence: AI saves recruiters ~20% of time

tip: After a campaign, ask an AI tool to boil candidate feedback comments down to the top three themes.
why_it_works: It turns a pile of written comments into a few clear takeaways you can actually act on.
evidence: AI augments analysis tasks effectively

tip: Plan to track the diversity of your applicants, your shortlist and your final hire separately, and decide this up front.
why_it_works: Watching where different groups drop out shows you if your process is quietly being unfair to some people.
evidence: Stage-level monitoring is the recommended bias-audit approach

tip: Every few months, ask your hiring managers how happy they are with recent hires and the process.
why_it_works: Their honest feedback is one of the best early signs of whether your hiring is actually working.
evidence: 44% of TA pros use manager satisfaction to judge how well hires work out

tip: Track what you spend per hire who actually works out, not just per hire, by counting only those still doing well at 90 days.
why_it_works: It shows when money is being wasted on hires who do not stick around.
evidence: Two-category quality framework

tip: Put the apply button right near the top of the ad so people see it straight away on any device.
why_it_works: If people have to hunt for how to apply, many give up, especially on a phone.
evidence: Mobile-first design lifts completion

tip: Let people apply without making them create an account first.
why_it_works: Forcing people to sign up is one of the quickest ways to lose good candidates before they finish.
evidence: Account creation is a top abandonment trigger

tip: If your ad has been live for two weeks with no applicants, rewrite and repost it rather than leaving it up.
why_it_works: A stale ad keeps underperforming, so refreshing it stops you wasting more budget on something that is not working.
evidence: Recurring ad performance optimisation tactic

tip: For high-volume roles, give each recruiter one campaign to focus on at a time instead of juggling several.
why_it_works: Focusing on one role at a time fills it faster and leads to better hires than splitting attention.
evidence: Capacity research shows reqs-per-recruiter discipline

tip: Hold a short 20-minute weekly catch-up with the hiring manager to review candidates and make decisions.
why_it_works: Regular quick check-ins keep things moving and stop good candidates going cold while everyone waits.
evidence: Cadence-based reviews accelerate hiring
